title: Dog Abdominal Pain: What to Do When Your Dog's Stomach Hurts
meta_desc: Is your dog showing signs of pain when their stomach is touched? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Abdominal pain in dogs can range from minor gas to a life-threatening emergency; contact a veterinarian within 24 hours, or seek immediate emergency care if your dog has a hard stomach, unproductive vomiting, or extreme lethargy.

What is abdominal pain in dogs and how do I recognize the symptoms?

Abdominal pain, or tenderness when the stomach is touched, occurs when your dog experiences discomfort in the area between their chest and pelvis. This condition often signals underlying inflammation, infection, or a physical blockage. Common signs include:

  • Tensing of the muscles (guarding)
  • Whining when the area is touched
  • Snapping or aggression when you attempt to pet them
  • Visible tenderness in the gastrointestinal tract or surrounding organs

Is my dog's abdominal pain a veterinary emergency?

While some cases are caused by minor gas or dietary indiscretion, the urgency level is generally Medium. Follow these guidelines for seeking care:

  • Contact a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours if the pain persists.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your dog's stomach feels hard or bloated.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if they are unsuccessfully trying to vomit (dry heaving).
  • Seek immediate emergency care if they are exhibiting extreme lethargy.

How can I use photos and videos to help triage my dog?

Providing visual evidence to a veterinarian or triage specialist is incredibly helpful for determining the severity of the issue. Be sure to capture:

  • Posture videos: Record your dog in the "prayer position" (front legs down, rear up), which is a clear sign of abdominal distress.
  • Swelling photos: Take clear photos of any visible swelling on the belly.
  • Skin discoloration: Document any changes in skin color on the abdominal area to help identify the type of issue your dog is facing.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Abdominal pain in dogs can be acute or subacute, characterized by whining, and abnormal postures such as outstretched forelimbs with the sternum on the floor and raised hindlimbs, or a "praying" posture. Diagnosis is usually based on a thorough history, clinical findings, and response to symptomatic treatment. A specific diagnosis should be sought if the animal has had access to foreign objects or toxins, if clinical signs do not resolve within 2 days of symptomatic therapy, if hematemesis or melena are present, if the animal is systemically unwell, or if abnormalities are noted on abdominal palpation. Diagnostic testing may include a CBC, serum biochemical profile, urinalysis, specific clinicopathologic testing, and diagnostic imaging. Relief of abdominal pain via analgesics should be considered when the pain is reflexively affecting other body systems, such as cardiovascular collapse. Replacement of fluids and electrolytes is necessary when dehydration and electrolyte and acid-base imbalance occur.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: What are the common signs of stomach pain in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include a hunched back, shivering, whining when picked up, restlessness, and a sudden lack of interest in food.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human medication for stomach pain?
faqs.answer: No. Many human pain relievers, such as ibuprofen or aspirin, are toxic to dogs and can cause severe internal bleeding or organ failure.

faqs.question: How can I make my dog comfortable while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog in a quiet, confined space and discourage them from jumping or running. Do not force them to eat or drink until you have spoken with a professional.
